item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you referring to this one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Note 19-A3: Ariella █████ went missing from [REDACTED] on 7/██/19██ at age 7 before being discovered ten years later in the cellar of a neighbor. Further details are unavailable due to an ongoing investigation."
This note is written right before the details of the discovery are included in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still haunted by this one especially when I see age progression pictures of missing people

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ThOuGh i hAvE To tRaVeL FaR

item.title: Re: Mistake Spotted.
item.content:encoded: This suddenly makes the whole thing make way more sense. At one point I thought the original WAS the neighbour, who wasn't identified because she doesn't look like the age-progressed photos, but the copy does, because of how the meme draws on the idealized memory of the girl rather than how people actually age. The middle of the article REALLY needs a correctional rewrite because the idea is awesome but the text is incomprehensibly (and, as pointed out above, anachronically) structured.

item.title: Class change?
item.content:encoded: It seems like the lack of precise knowledge behind this makes it fall into Euclid class pretty specifically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude, my name is Lee…

Dude, you just creeped me the f*** out.

Take a 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Ariella █████ and her mother have been confirmed as members of "Remember Ari"."

That is one hell of a meme. At least I think it's a meme. It spreads around between people in the form of an idea.

item.title: Re: Mistake Spotted.
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that whole part is so awkwardly worded I can't make sense of it.

I think the author's saying that the original girl was found 10 years after disappearing, but that a fake copy of her was found 9 years after disappearing, and that that one looked more like her age-progressed photos than the real girl. But for some reason those two events are listed non-chronologically. Then, for the rest of the text, the author repeatedly switches between describing one girl, then the other, without ever making it clear who's being talked about at any given time.

Adding to the confusion is the fact that both girls were found in separate cellars (the real girl was found 10 years later in a neighbor's cellar, while the fake girl was found 9 years later in a cellar 70km from where she disappeared).

This is what I understood, but I'm not even sure I'm right.

Really confused, I won't downvote this because I think there's a great SCP somewhere in there, but I won't upvote either until this is made clearer.

item.title: Mistake Spotted.
item.content:encoded:
Spotted an Mistake for you.
Note 19-A3 stated that she went missing for 10 years.
However, the next description said she was found 9 years later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saw the title and couldn't help but think "Run you clever boy".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am very, very confused, because Note 19-A3 seems to contradict itself multiple times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thing is, there are lots of things in containment, even Safe-class, that would become horrifically dangerous if a breach were to occur. Thing is, they're contained. The Keter cakes are Keter because they're hard to contain; this just requires two people watching the girl, which isn't really undue procedure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way I see it, the Keter is simply because containment breach could lead to an exponential duplication of the girl, which can be dangerous if the crowd of clones suddenly panics, and downright catastrophic if their number increase to quickly, much like the Keter cakes.

I wonder if the duplicates are affected by their own effect (does an instance of the SCP count as an observer with regard to the duplication of another instance).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't intended it to be anything other than something to get around posting extraneous/redundant information akin to a police report

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't rate because I really liked the idea, but found the "pending further investigation" stuff to be kind of a cheat and unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it; how is this Keter? If it's spreading, the SCP should be the phenomenon, not the girl. The remembrance group almost comes off as anomalous, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is completely and utterly different from SCP-2288. This is as much related to 2288 as 106 is related to 173 (creepy lookin things wot teleport). Literally the only common factor between them is teenage girls, and duplication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't find this to be different enough from 2288 to make up for its somewhat unremarkable execution. -1 from me in its current form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made my day by showing that people still care about Futurama :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's different enough to stand on its own.

I am not making a Copy of A joke. I will prevent myself from making a Copy of A joke. It will not be funny. It will most likely take the form of a CSI sunglasses joke. No matter how perfect the setup, I will not make that joke. I will resist…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oi Andy, didn't you re-write this already?

E: Wait, I don't get it. Is this related to SCP-2288 or is it the same thing. Because SCP-2288 is still there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like others said, this took a while to get, but once I did I loved it. Here is an enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ur at least partly right, mostly with the second half

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to reread this three times to understand it, but when I did, I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok. Somehow, children, possibly being kidnapped by the same guy, are becoming physical manifestations of the very idea of a meme, and are also creating memetic hazards that demand the remembrance of the children. That's both creepy and sad. I saw this in chat, but didn't quite understand it, and so refrained from reviewing it, but I get it now. Take a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Wait. So is the incorrectly formatted title intentional? I just changed it, but if it's a format-screw thing, feel free to revert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is what I had posted months ago as SCP-2288 as a version of "Copy of A". I added more stuff and hopefully made it more clear and such.

Title is meant to be said like this.
